title: HTML Attributes
description: Core rules for writing, reading, and safely using HTML attributes.

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
Objectives
  • Write valid HTML attributes using correct syntax
  • Use global attributes like id, class, style, and title on any element
  • Apply element-specific attributes such as src, href, and alt
  • Use boolean and data-* attributes correctly
  • Avoid common attribute security mistakes

sections.title: Attribute Syntax
sections.lesson:
Attribute Syntax
An attribute adds extra information to a tag. It goes inside the opening tag as name="value".
Attribute names are case-insensitive, but values belong in quotes.
<img src="cat.jpg" alt="A cat">
Here src and alt are both attributes of the img tag.

quiz.question: Where does an attribute go in an HTML element?
options: Inside the opening tag, after the name
Inside the closing tag, before the slash
Between the opening tag and closing tag
On its own line above the tag

sections.title: Global Attributes
sections.lesson:
Global Attributes
Global attributes work on almost any HTML element.
  • id — unique identifier for one element
  • class — reusable label for styling or scripting
  • style — inline CSS for that element
  • title — tooltip text shown on hover
<p id="intro" class="highlight-text" title="Read this first">Welcome</p>

quiz.question: Which global attribute must be unique on the page?
options: id
class
style
title

sections.title: Element-Specific Attributes
sections.lesson:
Element-Specific Attributes
Some attributes only make sense on certain tags. src and alt belong to img; href belongs to a.
<a href="page.html">Go</a> <img src="logo.png" alt="Site logo">
alt is not optional — it describes the image for screen readers and shows if the image fails to load.

quiz.question: What happens if logo.png fails to load in this code?
options: The alt text "Site logo" displays instead
The page stops loading entirely
The img tag is removed automatically
The browser shows a blank white box

sections.title: Boolean Attributes
sections.lesson:
Boolean Attributes
A boolean attribute is on just by being present — it needs no value.
<input type="text" disabled> <input type="checkbox" checked>
Writing disabled="disabled" or just disabled means the same thing. Leaving it out means the feature is off.

quiz.question: How do you make a checkbox checked by default?
options: Add the checked attribute, no value
Set checked equal to false
Add a value of checked
Set default equal to true

sections.title: data-* Custom Attributes
sections.lesson:
data-* Custom Attributes
A data-* attribute stores extra info on an element without showing it to the user.
<li data-user-id="42" data-role="admin">Criscel</li>
Any name after data- works, as long as it's lowercase. The browser ignores it visually — it's just stored on the tag.

quiz.question: What does a data-* attribute do to the page a user sees?
options: Nothing visible — it stores info on the tag
It shows the value as bold text
It adds a number badge next to the item
It changes the list item's color

sections.title: Quoting Rules
sections.lesson:
Quoting Rules
Attribute values should always be in quotes — double quotes are the standard in this course.
<p class="note">Correct</p> <p class=note>Works but avoid</p>
Skip quotes only when the value has no spaces — but always quoting keeps your HTML consistent and avoids bugs.

quiz.question: Why should attribute values always be quoted?
options: Values with spaces break without quotes
Quotes make the browser load faster
Unquoted attributes are removed by HTML5
Quotes are required for every HTML tag

sections.title: Combining Attributes — Full Example
sections.lesson:
Combining Attributes — Full Example
Real elements usually carry several attributes at once, in any order.
<input type="email" id="user-email" class="form-control" placeholder="you@example.com" required>
This input has five attributes: a type, an id for scripting, a class for styling, a placeholder hint, and the boolean required.

quiz.question: In the input above, which attribute makes the field mandatory?
options: required
type
placeholder
id

sections.title: Security & Best Practice
sections.lesson:
Security & Best Practice
Never build attribute values by pasting in raw user input — it opens the door to HTML injection.
<!-- Risky: unescaped user input --> <div title="<?php echo $_GET['name']; ?>"></div>
Escape special characters like <, >, and quotes before inserting outside data into an attribute value.

quiz.question: What is the main risk of inserting raw user input into an attribute?
options: It can let attackers inject malicious code
It slows down the page load time
It makes the HTML file larger
It breaks CSS styling on the element

sections.title: Recap
sections.lesson:
Recap
  • Attributes sit inside the opening tag as name="value" pairs
  • Global attributes like id, class, style, and title work almost anywhere
  • Some attributes, like src, href, and alt, belong to specific elements
  • Boolean attributes (disabled, checked, required) work by presence alone
  • data-* attributes store custom info on a tag without showing it to users
  • Always quote attribute values to avoid bugs
  • Elements can carry several attributes at once, in any order
  • Never insert raw user input into an attribute — escape it first
